text: This is Shannon, an open-source autonomous AI pentester that performs code analysis and executes live exploits using browser automation to find all kinds of security vulnerabilities from serverside request forgery to cross-sight scripting to SQL injection and much much more giving you a detailed comprehensive security report with zero false positives. But with the announcement of Claude Code Security and also the fact that Shannon is built on the Claude SDK, meaning you can't use your subscription, is there any point in learning at all that might not be around for that long? Hit subscribe and let's get into it. In one of my previous jobs, we'd pay thousands of dollars to external pen testers before a major release, only to find out there were bugs we needed to fix, and then we'd get it tested again, costing us lots of time and of course money. But this is exactly what Shannon exists to fix. You can run Shadowland as many times as you want. You can even put it in a CI/CD pipeline and run it automatically. And because it's open- source, it's completely free. Well, there is a paid version, which we'll talk about later. But as someone who's not a security expert, I'd rather run my project through Shannon than boot up Kali Linux. In fact, let's see Shannon in action. And temporal ensures durable your computer crashes midway through a pentest or you run out of clawed credits and need to top up, you don't lose any progress. Temporal remembers exactly where you left off and restarts Shannon from that checkpoint. Let me know in the comments if you want a dedicated video about Temporal, but it also orchestrates all of Shannon's phases and activities. And even though there are only five phases, a lot of things happen inside them. Let me show you. Starting with the pre-flight phase that makes sure API credentials are valid, Docker containers are ready, and the repo actually exists. Then the pre-recon stage which analyzes the code to understand how the app works. So architecture mapping entry points and security patterns. Next is the actual recon stage which is very different from the pre-recon because here playright is used to navigate through the app. So it will click buttons, fill in forms and use that to observe network requests, take screenshots, look at cookies, basically map out all the functionality of the app. And then phase four does five pipelines in parallel. So here we have injection related vulnerabilities and exports. Then cross- sight scripting vulnerabilities and exports, then authentication, server side request forgery, and finally we have authorization. So accessing privileged data or other people's information and all of this happens in parallel on five different agents [clears throat] for vulnerability and then another five for exploits. And finally we have phase five which compiles everything into a comprehensive pentest report by combining the last five checks. Speaking of report, let's see how our pentest is coming along. So after almost 2 and 1/2 hours the full process is complete and we can see here it started with the pre-flight validation before moving on to the pre-recon and then the recon agent and then here it runs all the vulnerability checks. So we've got the injection vulnerability agent cross-sight scripting authorization SSRF and you can see for some of these the green line is not solid. This is because it had to retry because I ran out of claw credits. So you can see there's a two here and for these ones there weren't any retry. So it may have been faster than 2 and 1/2 hours if it wasn't for these retries but I don't think it would have been less than two hours. So almost $60 in Claude credits was spent running this pen test which is cheaper than hiring a human tester but it's still a lot of money and I would have loved to use my clause pro or max subscription which would have made the whole thing a lot cheaper which is hopefully what clause code security will allow you to do when it's properly released unless the team at keygraph end up rewriting Shannon in something like the open AI agents SDK or use the VEL AI SDK which allows you to use many more models but overall if you're a startup up and don't want to spend a lot of money on a human pen tester, then Shannon is a good alternative.
